coredata.dc:title: A novel MnFe2O4@CDs material of dual behavior: photothermal conversion performance and fluorescent probe. Capture, detection, and degradation of Atrazine

coredata.dc:description: The detection and removal of Atrazine (ATZN), a pesticide of environmental concern, requires more efficient methods to facilitate its degradation. The adequate structural morphology, high specific surface area, great photothermal conversion performance, higher amount of oxygenated functional groups, and intense and stable fluorescence signals are considered as favorable properties of the materials used in the in the proposal of physicochemical methods for detection, capture and degradation of nitrogenous herbicides. Herein, the design, preparation, and characterization of MnFe2O4@CDs as a dual-functional material is reported. Due to its optical, photothermal, textural and surface chemistry properties, the material is able to capture, detect, and degrade the herbicide "Atrazine" in synthetic samples. The preparation of the proposed composites, formed by particles of bimetallic oxides (Mn-Fe2O4) and a covering of carbon dots (CDs), was confirmed using various characterization techniques including SEM, TEMHR, RAMAN, FTIR, UV-Visible, Photoluminescence, DRX, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, superficial chemistry, and photothermal. The study revealed that the ATZN capture occurs through hydrogen bonding interaction between −COOH and −COH functional groups exposed on CDs surface and amine groups −NH− of the herbicide. Furthermore, the CDs behave as fluorescent markers of single-channel employed to detect ATZN and to monitor the capture-degradation process. The MnFe2O4@CDs composite was integrated as the main thermoactive material into a photothermal reactor on which was incident a NIR laser. The high near-infrared absorption of the Mn-Fe2O4 particles resulting in an efficient photothermal conversion; which in turn, increase the temperature of the surrounding medium. The increase in temperature promotes the activation of persulfate (PS) at the interface of the MnFe2O4@CDs−PS system producing SO4 • − radicals as oxidizing agents of atrazine. Our results demonstrate that the process may effectively degrade 99 % of atrazine for a concentration of CATZN [20 mM], when NIR light irradiated by 45 min and the system reaches a temperature of ca. 53 ºC. Additionally, the degradation of ATZN was confirmed by analysis of total organic carbon (TOC). The fluorometric analytical assay by CDs-base fluorescence probes allowed following the FL signal at 520 nm(λexit = 375 nm) for the capture “turn on” and degradation “turn off” of atrazine. Finally, as a comparison, we highlight the significant efficiency shown by the process studied here, compared to other similar atrazine degradation processes previously reported.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image of spherical carbon structures identified in a sample of Carbon Dots material. The inset shows the fringe lattice spacing consistent with graphite. (b) Picture of CDs suspension upon excitation with 375 nm UV lamp.
(a) MnFe2O4 magnetic material, (b-e) SEM/EDS mapping of the MnFe2O4 material: (c) Mn (d) O and (e) Fe images.
(a) SEM, (b) EDAX, (c-g) SEM/EDS mapping of the MnFe2O4@CDs composites.
(a) Diffractogram and (b) Raman spectra of the MnFe2O4@CDs composites.

(a) average zeta potential, for the concentration rations (MnFe2O4: CDs) 1:1, 1:2 and 1:3 respectively (b) distribution function of ionization constants, (c) FTIR spectra, (d) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of MnFe2O4@CDs samples.

(a) Heating curve at different optical density of MnFe2O4 and MnFe2O4@CDs dry poweder, (b) Thermal mapping image of MnFe2O4@CDs dispersed in aqueous solution irradiated with 980 nm laser; the suspension was stirred with a magnetic plate and bar.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, MnFe2O4 and MnFe2O4@CDs materials. (b) FL spectra with different excitation wavelengths. Insert to figure shows the picture of MnFe2O4@CDs suspension upon excitation with 375 nm UV lamp and daylight. (c) Profile of the total integrated intensity of PL vs. excitation wavelength of MnFe2O4@CDs.

(a) FL emission spectra of MnFe2O4@CDs composites with the standard addition of ATZN at different concentrations from 0 mM at 22.5 mM. Insert to figure shows the plotted linear relationship between (F/F0) of FL emission peak intensity vs. lower concentrations of ATZN CATZN (0 μM – 250 μM). (b) Interference percentage as a function of (F/F0) emission peak intensity in presence of possible interfering analytes.

(a) Capture curve for addition of CATZN (0 mM – 22.5 mM); “turn on” of FL, (b) ATZN removal/degradation as a function of time using MnFe2O4@CDs composites; “turn off” of FL, (c) CDs-base photoluminescent probes visual changes appreciation of ATZN capture-degradation processes under excitation with a 365 nm UV lamp.

Changes in the porcentual total organic carbon (%TOC) removed as a function of degradation time. Sample of ATZN [20 mM] in triplicate under next conditions: PS [5.0 mM], T = 53 °C ± 2, pH= 8.0. Insert in the figure is shown the graphic of %TOC before and after ATZ degradation process.

Scheme of the mechanism of chemical and photothermal PS activation by MnFe2O4@CDs compound for atrazine degradation. Also shown the proposal of the main intermediate degradation products formed within degradation pathway of atrazine by the MnFe2O4@CDs-PS system.

(a) mass of the recovered Mn–Fe2O4 particles. (b) recorded temperature by photothermal conversion performance of the Mn–Fe2O4 particles. Percentage capacity of (c) ATZN capture and (d) ATZN degradation by the MnFe2O4@CDs composites. Data obtained during 5 cycles of the ATZN capture and degradation process.
Effect of PS concentration on the degradation of ATZN. Values recorded with the fluorometric assay for the detection of ATZN.
Main characteristics of ATZN degradation processes using iron materials and thermal option for activation of sulfate oxidant species.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detection and removal of Atrazine (ATZN), a pesticide of environmental concern, requires more efficient methods to facilitate its degradation. The adequate structural morphology, high specific surface area, great photothermal conversion performance, higher amount of oxygenated functional groups, and intense and stable fluorescence signals are considered as favorable properties of the materials used in the in the proposal of physicochemical methods for detection, capture and degradation of nitrogenous herbicides. Herein, the design, preparation, and characterization of MnFe2O4@CDs as a dual-functional material is reported. Due to its optical, photothermal, textural and surface chemistry properties, the material is able to capture, detect, and degrade the herbicide "Atrazine" in synthetic samples. The preparation of the proposed composites, formed by particles of bimetallic oxides (Mn-Fe2O4) and a covering of carbon dots (CDs), was confirmed using various characterization techniques including SEM, TEMHR, RAMAN, FTIR, UV-Visible, Photoluminescence, DRX, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, superficial chemistry, and photothermal. The study revealed that the ATZN capture occurs through hydrogen bonding interaction between −COOH and −COH functional groups exposed on CDs surface and amine groups −NH− of the herbicide. Furthermore, the CDs behave as fluorescent markers of single-channel employed to detect ATZN and to monitor the capture-degradation process. The MnFe2O4@CDs composite was integrated as the main thermoactive material into a photothermal reactor on which was incident a NIR laser. The high near-infrared absorption of the Mn-Fe2O4 particles resulting in an efficient photothermal conversion; which in turn, increase the temperature of the surrounding medium. The increase in temperature promotes the activation of persulfate (PS) at the interface of the MnFe2O4@CDs−PS system producing SO4 • − radicals as oxidizing agents of atrazine. Our results demonstrate that the process may effectively degrade 99 % of atrazine for a concentration of CATZN [20 mM], when NIR light irradiated by 45 min and the system reaches a temperature of ca. 53 ºC. Additionally, the degradation of ATZN was confirmed by analysis of total organic carbon (TOC). The fluorometric analytical assay by CDs-base fluorescence probes allowed following the FL signal at 520 nm(λexit = 375 nm) for the capture “turn on” and degradation “turn off” of atrazine. Finally, as a comparison, we highlight the significant efficiency shown by the process studied here, compared to other similar atrazine degradation processes previously reported.

ce:section.ce:para: Atrazine (6-chloro-N-ethyl-N -isopropyl-1,3,5-triazine-2,4-diamine) is an herbicide widely used in agriculture [1]. Due to its toxicity, relatively high stability in water and soil, atrazine (ATZN) is one of the most hazardous herbicides for the environment [2], human health [3], and workers along the food chain processes [4]. Also, due to the high probability of leakage and absorption by organic materials, including comestibles, and clay, atrazine has been quickly gone from a leading herbicide to an accepted dangerous surface and groundwater pollutant [5,6]. The environmental persistence of ATZN has been shown to be a very significant problem, which is associated to a continued release of ATZN from the soil into the groundwater combined with its low degradation in these aquifer layers. In such context, Vonberg et al. have monitored the contamination of groundwater in western Germany since 2014 and found atrazine present in groundwater after 20 years of banning its use [2]. ATZN can be transported to drinking water sources, making it the most commonly detected pesticide in surface water sources [7,8]. On human health, the possible consequences of being exposed to ATZN include effects on the expression of certain proteins, DNA damage, sperm mutagenesis, and disruption of endocrine hormones [9]. Therefore, numerous detection methods have been implemented to constantly improve the detection limit of ATZN. Moreover, the main conventional techniques for detection of ATZN in water matrices are gas chromatography (GC) [10,11] and high performance liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [7,12], but these analytical techniques have disadvantages such as sample preparation, separation steps and preconcentration of analyte that are necessary prior to the instrumental analyses; and moreover, they are very expensive, and although robust, it is difficult to deploy them in the field. Alternative options are the sensors and biosensors that have been developed and applied to detect the presence of herbicide in water [13,14]. Nanosensors and nanobiosensors, that is, sensors based on porous materials have the advantage of detecting substances at the micro and nanoscale. This is in part because the specific surface area increases with decreasing size, increasing the number of sites available for molecular interactions [15]. The performance of these devices also depends on the type of nanomaterials used to prepare the sensing surface. Among these nanomaterials are the carbon based [16,17], metallic particles [18,19], and conducting polymers [20], among others. Fluorescence sensing also has been an efficient detection method associated with high sensitivity, spatiotemporal resolution, and versatility [21,22]. One interesting alternative as fluorescent sensors are the carbon dots (CDs). CDs are considered as nanomaterials useful for fluorescent sensors and have been employed for the detection of few pesticides like sinapine [23], carbaryl [24], glyphosate [25], and atrazine [26] among others. More recently, the fluorescent (FL) probe in aqueous suspension of Carbon Dots, has gained attention due to its significant properties; such as, low cost, operational simplicity, real-time and visual detection, high sensitivity and selectivity [27]. Up to now, only a fluorescent and visual analytical method for herbicides detection like cymoxanil using a CDs fluorescent probe has been reported [28].

In addition to the interest on detecting and quantifying ATZN levels in both soil and water samples; the need and challenge arises on implementation of processes for removal and degrade ATZN. During the last years, some research groups have advocated for developing methods for ATZN removal from soil and water [1]. These include chemical and physicochemical, processes such as Fenton [29], photocatalysis [30,31], and nano-zero valent iron [32]. Recently, Baldissarelli et. al [33] suggested the use of physicochemical methods, i.e., techniques including oxidizing agents, or that generate a secondary oxidizing agent, should be efficient for soil remediation. These methods previously used for water, can be applied to soils or lixiviates from soils, with Fenton reactants suggested to be the most promising for the degradation of ATZN [33]. Hybrid materials have been proposed for the generation of oxidants. Wu et al. reported removing ATZN in water (46 μM) up to 92.1 % within 21 min using zero-valent iron (nZVI) and graphene (GR) composites in combination with persulfate, they found nZVI/GR-PS system to efficiently produce SO4 • − radicals [34]. In another study, Zhu et al. prepared Co-doped mesoporous FePO4 to activate peroximonosulfate and degradate 10 μM of ATZN also through the oxidation of the pesticide due to the effect of SO4 • − radicals [35]. The activation of persulfate by FeS2 has also been reported by Wang et al. by generating more radical species such as SO4 • − and O2 • − for the 93 μM solution of ATZN; about 70 % was degraded within 10 min [36]. Li et al. have demonstrated that Fe3O4/Peroximonosulfate (PMS) system in the presence of hydroxylamine efficiently degraded ATZN (23 μM) up to 94 % within 15 min [37]. Also, Truc et al., reported the photocatalytic ATZN degradation by the synthesized Cu-ZnO/g-C3N4, through the generation of hydroxyl and superoxide radicals proving 97 % of degradation of ATZN (100 ppm) within 120 min [38]. Similarly, Yang et al., have shown the advanced oxidation technology based on the visible light catalysis and nanocatalyst Co3O4/g-C3N4 synergistically to producer sulfate radicals to efficiently degrade and remove ATZN (50 μM), by 78.5 % after 35 min [39]. An advanced oxidation process was reported by Huang et al., and their results suggest that the UV–vis light drives the ZnCuFe2O4/sulfite system with photocatalytic ability to activate sulfite for the degradation of ATZN (4.4 μM), by 95 % in 30 min [40]. In another report Jiang et al., showed the formation of SO4 • − and OH• radicals for the degradation of ATZN; reaching up to 67.7 % removalof ATZN (50 ppm) at 50 °C within 240 min; solely through thermal-activation of the reaction medium without the use of other materials as activating agents [41].

In this context, another alternative for the ATZN degradation can be through the use of materials with photothermal properties that promote the temperature increase in the reaction system and this thermal effect improves the catalytic performance in the degradation process. For instance, Wang et al. reported the degradation of antibiotics in wastewater treatment using a photothermal-assisted photocatalyst system that uses graphene oxide as a photothermal substrate and g-C3N4 as a photocatalyst [42]. Similarly, Yang et al. used the core-shell material CoTiO3@MnO2 as a photothermal photocatalyst, which demonstrated excellent tetracycline (TC) degradation performance. The improved photocatalyst performance is due to the broad-spectrum absorption by CoTiO3 and the photothermal effect of MnO2 [43]. Recently, Liu et al. synthesized the MnFe2O4/ZnIn2S4 material, which demonstrated excellent performance in degrading TC and 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) solutions. In this case, the photothermocatalytic degradation was attributed to the synergism between the photothermal conversion by MnFe2O4 under near-infrared (NIR) irradiation at 808 nm; and the photocatalyst capacity shown by ZnIn2S4 [44].

Therefore, the main objective of the present study, was to stablish a novel MnFe2O4@CDs material, which shows efficient capacity for the capture, detection, and subsequent degradation of ATZN. Hence, the strategic design of the material has been considering that CDs are useful as fluorescent probe for its application in the development of analytical methods [27] for the detection of herbicides [28]. Additionally, for the first time we use the bimetallic oxide Mn-Fe2O4 material because it acts as a center which exhibits a strong absorption of light at near-infrared (IR) wavelength and higher photothermal conversion [45]. In this study, we show that the simultaneous and continuous process carried out by the MnFe2O4@CDs material in aqueous suspension for the capture, detection and degradation of ATZN occurs through two main phenomena: (i) “Turns-on” the carbon-dots based fluorescent signal due to the addition of ATZN. ATZN is captured by the hydrogen bonding interaction between surface oxygenated groups (−COOH and −COH) on CDs and the –NH– groups of ATZN. The interactions allow us to measure the CDs fluorescence signal at 520 nm, under photoexcitation at 370 nm. (ii) The degradation of the captured ATZN is carried out by an oxidation process. This phenomenon occurs by the high near-infrared absorption of Mn-Fe2O4 particles that result in efficient photothermal conversion that generates heat, which activates persulfate (PS) generating SO4 •− radicals at the interface of the MnFe2O4@CDs–PS system.This paper reports the most influential parameters in the process development, like pH, reaction time and temperature, concentration of MnFe2O4, CDs, ATZN and PS. Also, the photothermal parameters of power density and irradiation time with a NIR laser were investigated. The efficient degradation, up to 99 % for 20 mM ATZN within 45 min in synthetic samples. This high activity is attributed to the characteristics of the MnFe2O4@CDs material, such as, structural morphology and elemental composition; mesoporosity and BET active surface area. These characteristics are obtained due to the co-precipitation of carbonaceous materials on the metal particles, which in turn allows for a greater number of oxygenated active sites capable of significantly bonding with ATZN. When the maximum concentration of ATZN is captured, the temperature is increased up to 53 ºC by induced photothermal conversion to generate the production of SO4 •− radicals that oxidize and degrade ATZN. Finally, the CDs-base fluorescence probes, allowed an easy colorimetric and fluorometric visualization of the capture-detection and degradation of ATZN by measuring the CDs fluorescence signal at 520 nm(λex 370 nm). This fluorometric assay allows us to demonstrate the minimum detection of atrazine with LOD = 126 nM ± 0.06 and the maximum capture capacity of the herbicide up to CATZN [22.5 mM], simultaneously.

Iron nitrate nonahydrate [Fe(NO3)3∙9H2O 99 %], ammonium hydroxide [NH4OH 99 %], monobasic phosphate (KH2PO4), dibasic phosphate (K2HPO4), ethylene glycol [C2H4(OH)2 99.5 %], zinc nitrate Zn(NO3)2, calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium sulphate (MgSO4) analytical grade were bought from J.T. Baker. Citric acid [C6H8O7 99.5 %], Glucose monohydrate [C6H12O6·H2O 99.5 %], manganese (II) nitrate tetrahydrate [Mn(NO3)2∙4 H2O 97 %], atrazine (C8H14N5Cl, 99.0 %), sodium persulfate (Na2S2O8, 99.5 %), cymoxanil (C7H10N4O3, analytical standard), methiocarb (C11H15NO2S), TOC standard 1000mg/L were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All other reagents were of analytical grade and prepared in Milli-Q water (18 MΩ·cm). Tubes with filter units for centrifuge with 3KD permittivity were purchased from Merck Millipore. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) was prepared by mixing K2HPO4 and KH2PO4 and was used for pH adjustment. Synthetic samples were prepared varying the concentration of ATZN and adding ions and molecules that probably are found in real samples. The species added as possible interferents of ATZN, were: PO4 3-, NO3 -, Cl-, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cymoxanil, Methiocarb, and Imidacloprid.

The preparation of the composites was carried out through the synthesis of Mn-Fe2O4 and CDs in parallel. The doped Mn-Fe2O4 was synthetized by the Pechini method [46]. In an usual synthesis a quantity of citric acid in ratio 1.5:1 w/w with Iron Nitrate nonahydrate were dissolved in 1:1 vol water: ethanol at 30° C and stirring 250 rpm. Thereafter a certain amount of manganese nitrate (10 %) was added to the solution. Then, the solution was heated up to 90° C until half the liquid was evaporated. Finally, ethylene glycol in a relation 1:3 (wt/wt) was added to the solution. Ammonium hydroxide was used to adjust the pH until obtaining a complete transparent solution and heated up to 150° C to produce the polymerization obtaining a light brown gel. Finally, the gel was combusted and annealed in alumina crysol at 500 and 700° C to remove the organic waste, obtaining a brownish powder.

CDs were synthetized through a hydrothermal carbonization method, using ascorbic acid and glucose as precursors. 1.5 g ascorbic acid and 1.5 g glucose were dissolved in 500 mL of deionized water with the aid of stirring, and then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and further heated to 170 °C for 12 h [47]. The obtained solution was concentrated to 60 mL in a drying oven at 70 °C and filtered by dialysis using 3KD centrifugal filter units for two days.

The wrapping of MnFe2O4 particles with CDs was carried out in the aqueous suspension of CDs and MnFe2O4 where a 1:1 (vol/vol) ratio of both was added to yield a uniform mixture solution. The final concentration of CDs was fixed to 1.2 mg mL−1, and the MnFe2O4 was regulated from 0.05 to 0.26 mM in an ethanol:water (1:1) v/v solution. The pH of mixture solution was adjusted from 5.0 to 8.5 using PBS solution. The glass vial was immersed in the High-Powered Ultrasonic Cleaner (Sper Scientific-10004) for sonication for approximately 15 min intervals up to one hour.

The morphology and elemental analysis (by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, EDS) were carried out by using both a dual beam scanning electron microscope (FIB/SEM,FEI-Helios Nanolab 600). The powder samples were placed on gold discs suitable for SEM imaging. Transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL 200 CX). Liquid samples were placed on carbon grids. UV–vis absorption spectra (DR/UV–vis) were recorded using an Agilent Cary 6000i UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer, equipped with an integrating sphere. The BET surface area, pore volume and pore size distribution were determined by adsorption-desorption N2 isotherms at 77 K using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 equipment. A sample of each material was placed on a glassy substrate for Raman spectroscopy analysis using the InVia Microraman: Renishaw Spectrometer equipped with 532 nm and 633 nm lasers, Notch holographic filters and scattered light photodetector. Structural analysis was performed using a Bruker D2 Phaser diffractometer, with Cu K
α radiation (1.54184 Å), at a step of 0.05° and 0.05 s per step. The FT-IR study was carried out with an ISASA-FTIR spectrometer coupled to a microscope with a frequency range between 4000 and 400 cm−1. The titrations curves were determined with a Mettler Toledo - Titration Excellence T70. 150 mg MnFe2O4@CDs were added to a bottle containing 20 mL solution of 0.1 M NaCl solution as electrolyte. These bottles were stirred for 24 h and then adjusted to pH 11 with 0.1 M NaOH. From the previous study, the proton binding curves and pka distribution plot for the CDs sample were obtained by using the SAEIUS-pK-Dist software [48]. Analysis of Total organic carbon (TOC) was carried out using a TOC-5050A analyzer (Shimadzu, Japan).

The photoluminescence response was analyzed in the spectral range of ʎEm 400–700 nm, by exciting the sample in the ʎEx of 350–400 nm range. For this, a Newport brand Hg lamp with an output power of 350 W coupled to an Oriel 74125 Newport monochromator was used as an excitation source.

The setup used for the photo-thermo-degradation of ATZN is shown in the supplementary information (Figure SI-8) where the suspension of MnFe2O4@CDs with ATZN was irradiated, with the ATZN concentration ranging from 0 mM to 22.5 mM. A 750 mW NIR diode laser at 980 nm was the irradiation source. Irradiance studies were performed by focusing the spot on the center of the suspension and monitoring the temperature with a Fluke Ti300 thermal camera and keeping it under constant stirring at room temperature. Aliquots were taken every 15 min, which were reserved for later characterization.

All experiments were carried out in a 100 mL reactor flask with moderate magnetic stirrer. The reaction was started by adding a specified amount of MnFe2O4@CDs composite to 50 mL of mixture solution containing PS in a concentration range from 1.0 to 7.0 mM in a phosphate buffer (PBS) at pH 8.0. A standard addition method was used to consecutively add ATZN atrazine in a concentration range from 2.5 μM to 22.5 mM with the goal of capturing and detecting ATZN. After 20 min of reaction at 25 °C for each aliquot of ATZN, 2 mL samples were collected and analyzed by UV–vis and photoluminescence (FL) spectroscopy. Subsequently, after standardizing the aforementioned procedure, the influence of the interferents on the capture and detection of the ATZN was evaluated. Thus, a set of aqueous suspensions of the MnFe2O4@CDs material was prepared by integrating ATZN in the entire concentration range from 2.5 μM to 22.5 mM. At the same time, aliquots of double the concentration of each of the interferents (PO4 3-, NO3 -, Cl-, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cymoxanil, and Methiocarb, Imidacloprid) were added. FL emission spectra at 520 nm(λex = 370 nm) were measured to interpret the selectivity and sensitivity of the ATZN capture and detection method. Moreover, in the second stage, during the ATZN degradation process, the following parameters were evaluated: light source-sample distance, IR laser power, and sample temperature with respect to time. Aliquots were taken at 15-min intervals, which were analyzed by fluorescence and TOC.

The carbonaceous nanomaterials (Carbon Dots) were synthesized by the “bottom-up” route in a simple hydrothermal process (illustrated in Supplementary Information Figure SI-1). The CDs mechanism of formation starts with the decomposition of the components such as glucose and ascorbic acid [49] which can be converted into CDs under of the next stages: (i) Condensation, such as amidation [50], aldo-condensation [51]. (ii) Self-polymerization and carbonization. The carbon core grow at high temperature with the continuous carbonization and formed nanocrystalline CDs [52]. (iii) Surface functionalization can be directed with precursors containing, e.g., oxygenated groups, can functionalize the surface of CDs to improve the photoluminescent characteristics and their hydrophilic nature [53]. Using this method, we managed to obtain CDs with high water solubility and brighter blue emission when excited with a 365 nm UV lamp as can be seen in Fig. 1b. This stable aqueous suspension is due to the surface chemistry of the particles. Essentially the most abundant functional groups in CDs are oxygenated such as -COH and -COOH [54]. In our case we propose a possible mechanism of formation of CDs, which was supported by HRTEM results (See the details in Supplementary Information Figure SI-2). The images were obtained at different reaction periods (3, 6, 9, 10, 11 and 12 h) during the synthesis of CDs. In this way we show the formation of the spheric nanocrystalline structures of CDs.

By transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) a sample of CDs synthesized by the bottom-up hydrothermal route was observed. In the Fig. 1a, corresponding HRTEM images shows the typical spherical morphology of nanometric CDs with high graphitic and graphene composition, uniformly dispersed with no aggregation phenomenon [54,55]. By zooming in slightly on the center of one of the carbon spheres, it is possible to identify crystalline structures corresponding to a series of lattice fringes whose spacing is ≈ 0.232 nm, typical features to the graphite (100) crystal plane, which indicates that the CDs synthesized are crystalline graphite structures [56]. The histogram plot of the size distribution of CDs confirms that the diameter of the particles is located between 2 and 5 nm for a homogeneous population of nanoparticles (Figure SI-3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM/EDS analysis of binary oxides material “MnFe2O4”
ce:section.ce:para: Iron—manganese oxide particles have been prepared by Pechini method [46], which fundamentally consists of submitting the mixture of metallic salts and solvents to consecutive increases in temperature to obtain the corresponding composites (See the details in Supplementary Information Figure SI-4). The preparation process, which begins by dissolving citric acid and iron in a mixture (water: ethanol) under stirring at 30° C. As a second step the manganese nitrate salt was added to the former solution, heating up to 90° C until half the liquid phase evaporated. In the next step, the ethylene glycol was added to the solution of metal’ precursors heating up to 150° C to achieve complete chelation and polyesterification by solvent evaporation. In the last step, a brown light viscous gel was obtained that quickly transformed into dark-brown amorphous resin. The resulting resin was calcined from 500 until 700º C to remove all organic materials, obtaining a dark-brown powder. The powder was coded as MnFe2O4.

A sample of the resultant MnFe2O4 magnetic material ( Fig. 2a) was characterized by SEM/EDS. In Fig. 2b, shows that the MnFe2O4 material have a granular morphology, made up of particles of nanometric diameter; some agglomerates are visible on the film. The energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping results are presented in Fig. 2(c-e), where the images are consistent with a homogeneous distribution of the elements Mn, Fe and O, which indicates that the Fe−Mn binary oxides material was successfully formed by the Pechini method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and composition analysis of the MnFe2O4@CDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The well-defined MnFe2O4@CDs composites were prepared by a single step by using High-Powered Ultrasonic controlled method. The surface morphology, elemental composition, and colorimetric mapping of the prepared MnFe2O4@CDs materials were examined by SEM/EDS. Fig. 3(a) shows the SEM micrographs of the MnFe2O4@CDs powder samples, which are composed by spherical particles with nanometric sizes. These spheres are interfacially connected to each other indicating that the coalescence mechanism might have been the driving force to get the transition metals oxide mixture [57,58]. The energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping exhibits sphere that reveals an elemental distribution of Fe, Mn, and O, embedded or coated with a layer of low-density C element consistent with the smaller CDs depositing on the surface of the MnFe2O4 (Fig. 3d-g). In addition, Fig. 3b shows the elemental analysis by EDAX of the MnFe2O4@CDs material which exhibits the presence of iron, manganese, oxygen, and carbon in its corresponding spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural analysis of the MnFe2O4 and MnFe2O4@CDs materials by Raman and XRD spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The crystalline structure of MnFe2O4 was investigated by powder XRD. Fig. 4a shows the x-ray diffraction pattern of the MnFe2O4 sample. Maxima with different widths are observed, suggesting a mixture of two phases. The diffractogram shows the "thin" peaks (+, blue) correspond to the rhombohedral phase of Fe2O3 according to PDF 33–664; whereas the "wide" peaks (*, red) correspond to the cubic phase of MnFe2O4 according to the chart JCPDS 10–0319. This last phase is likely what provides the sample its magnetic properties, because Fe2O3 nanostructures have lower residual magnetization and coercivity than MnFe2O4 [59,60]. While incorporation of Mn into Fe3O4 is possible [61], we do not observe this phase. Further studies are necessary regarding the magnetic properties of our materials, because of the interest in tunning the magnetic properties with composition. In this work, we use the magnetic properties as to provide a physical separation from the aqueous mixture. The crystal size estimation for both phases was carried out using the Scherrer equation [62], obtaining an approximate size of 49 nm for the hematite phase and 34 nm for the manganese ferrite phase.

The MnFe2O4@CDs composites were further examined by Raman spectroscopy and the results are shown in Fig. 4b. The peaks at 592 cm−1 and 698 cm−1 are assigned to MnFe2O4 materials. According to previous studies, only such bands were assigned to the introduction of Mn in the Fe2O4 structure. The peak at ∼ 698 cm−1 was assigned to the symmetric stretching vibration Mn-O of the MnO6 group, and the peak located at ∼ 592 cm−1 could be attributed to the Mn-O stretching in the basal plane of the MnO6 sheet [63]. Contrasting the MnFe2O4 sample with MnFe2O4@CDs composites, the spectrum shows four peaks, as mentioned, the bands at 592 cm-1 and 698 cm-1 are assigned to the MnFe2O4 materials core. But also, we can see the characteristic D and G typical bands belonging to the shell of carbonous nanomaterials. The D band appears close to 1342 cm−1 and it is related to the first-order scattering zone, caused by disorder of defects in the sp2 carbon atoms, such as edge atoms (i.e. hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl groups), point defects, and decrease in particle size [64]. The G band nearly at 1577 cm−1 is considered as a feature band of the hexagonal graphite or graphene lattice, which is due to the C−C stretching mode of the C sp2−bonded [55]. The intensity ratio between the D and G bands reveals the density of defects in the graphitic lattice. The ratio of intensity I
D
/I
G in MnFe2O4@CDs composites is 0.98 a little higher compared to that of the graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets whose I
D
/I
G ration value is about 0.96 [46,65]. We attribute this change to the presence of large amounts of oxygen-containing functional groups on edge of the CDs, which causes an increase in the D band signal. The above confirms the composition of the crystalline structure of MnFe2O4@CDs composed of particles of bimetallic oxides (Mn-Fe2O4) and a covering of carbon dots (CDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure, surface properties and textural characterization of the MnFe2O4@CDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Syntheses of NPs of MnFe2O4 with a carbonous covering by conventional methods (i.e. Graphene) require as the first step, the surface modification of the particles with positively charged functional groups to attract negatively charge graphene oxide sheets [66,67]. Here, we demonstrate the preparation of the MnFe2O4 particles coated with densely packed CDs nanomaterials (Fig. 3) in a single step by using high-powered ultrasonic cleaner in an aqueous medium at pH ≈ 6; considering this condition the most appropriate to energetically favor the interactions between the MnFe2O4 material and the CDs. The pH value was obtained from measurements of the zeta potential as a function of pH for each of the materials (Figure SI-5). The process to produce MnFe2O4@CDs composites occurs because Fe−Mn binary oxides particles has a zeta potential (ζ) value of + 7.3 mV at a pH(ζ) ≈ 6.2, similar to previously reported values [68]. As can be seen in Fig. 5a, the potential difference (ζ) for MnFe2O4 bare particles is higher than that of oxygen-contain functional groups in CDs and whose value is −16.3 mV [28,54] over the pH range of 6.0 at 7.2. This fact indicates that there is an electrostatic attraction between MnFe2O4 and the CDs, favoring the formation of the MnFe2O4@CDs materials [45]. At the same time, as the concentration of the CDs increases during the preparation of the MnFe2O4@CDs materials, the value of their zeta potential also becomes more negative in the order of −24 mV, −30 mV, and −37 mV for the concentration rations (MnFe2O4: CDs) of 1:1, 1:2 and 1:3, respectively (Fig. 5a). This evident and substantial zeta potential changing values is, in turn, associated with the increase of CDs layers and, to the increase of oxygen- containing groups on the surface of the composite. To demonstrate this, studies of the surface chemistry of the MnFe2O4@CDs composite were carried out making use of the Jagiello-Sederich theorical-experimental method [54].

The proton binding curve, ion released (Q) vs. the pH (Figure. SI-6), was obtained and shows that the surface of CDs coating becomes negative when it loses protons (
− COO
−
e.g.) in the titrating solution to basic pH. This ionization behavior is related to the dissociation of acidic functional groups such as carboxyl, carbonyl and phenolic groups. A computational procedure was applied on proton binding curves, Q(pH), to obtain the distribution function of pKa’s. Fig. 5b shows the distribution function(pKa), f(pKa) corresponding to the functional groups mostly exposed by the CDs. The two first ionization constants with pKa 3.8 and 6.3 are associated to carboxylic groups (−COOH). The third and fourth peak at pKa 8.3 and 10.2 indicates the existence of hydroxyl phenolic groups (−COH) [54]. These results are in agreement with FTIR spectra of CDs, MnFe2O4 material, and the MnFe2O4@CDs composite, which are shown in Fig. 5c. Regarding carbonaceous nanomaterial (CDs), the peak near 3428 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of −OH bond, the peak at 2810 cm−1 correspond to aromatic stretching vibration of C−H bond, and the absorption peak at 1628 cm−1 originates from vibration of CO bond in carboxyl groups. An adsorption at 1350 cm−1 is assigned to CC bond stretching vibration, and the peak between 673 cm−1 and 900 cm−1 may belong to either an ester or ether C−O mode from carboxylic groups [28,54]. The MnFe2O4 peaks were explored at 528 cm−1which correspond to stretching vibration of Fe–O bonds [68]. The less intensity peak at 434 cm−1 was assigned for low crystalline ferrihydrite or amorphous iron (III)-hydroxide [69], and the sharp peak al 662 cm−1 was attributed to the presence of –Mn in iron oxide [57]. In this spectrum did not observed the feature peak ascribed to magnetite (Fe3O4) at 580 cm−1 [70]. In this regard, we believe that this is due to the majority formation of the crystalline phase of the MnFe2O4 material. Finally the MnFe2O4@CDs composite showed the tow escential bands at 528 cm−1 and at 662 cm−1 associated to stretching vibration of Fe–O and Mn–FeO bonds respectively. Also within the same spectrum, we confirmed the presence of the coating CDs on the MnFe2O4 particles where the sharp bands located at 3428 cm−1 and at 3216 cm−1 are assigned to hydroxyl groups O–H stretching vibrations possibly associated with phenolic-hydroxyl and carboxyl-hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CDs [71]. The peak at 2500 cm−1 was ascribed to carboxil acids (–COOH) [54]. The less sharp intensity peaks at 1630 cm−1 and 1348 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of the CO and CC groups [57]. The peaks between 1045 y 970 were attributed to the alkoxy C–O stretching and O–H bending vibration [54].

The surface contains oxygenated functional groups (−COOH and −COH) that promote the interaction and formation of hydrogen bonds between MnFe2O4@CDs and amine groups (−NH) of the ATZN favoring the capture and detection of this type of amino herbicides in an aqueous medium at a slightly basic pH ≈ 8.5 (Figure SI-4), because under these conditions the ATZ has an approximate zeta potential (ζ) of + 11.0 mV [28]. For ATZN to bind to the materials at relevant rates, it is essential that the active surface area of the MnFe2O4@CDs materials increase. The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm of the MnFe2O4@CDs composites are shown in Fig. 5d. According to the IUPAC, the N2 adsorption/desorption analysis exhibited a IV isotherm type [68,72]. From these isotherm profiles, the BET area values of 36, 53, y 61 m2 · g−1 were obtained for each MnFe2O4@CDs composite, depending on the larger amount of carbonaceous nanomaterial for the ratios (MnFe2O4: CDs) 1:1, 1:2 and 1:3 respectively. These values are up to 7 times higher than the bare material of MnFe2O4 (8.7 m2 · g−1). The MnFe2O4@CDs composites exhibited general features of mesoporous materials, which are materials with pore width between 2 nm – 50 nm; data given by the nature of the type of isotherm IV [68,72]. The pore with and total pore volume was calculated with the BJH (Barret-Joyner-Halenda) equation, and the correspond pore volume vs. pore width plot (Figure SI-7), indicates that the materials MnFe2O4: CDs with 1:1, 1:2 and 1:3 ratios have pores with diameters between 4 nm and 50 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal performance of MnFe2O4 particles and MnFe2O4@CDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: A series of MnFe2O4 (0.26 mM) colloidal suspension of particles were prepared to study its photothermal behavior. The samples were tested by a 980 nm laser radiation with an output power density ranging from 0 W/mm2 to 4.5 W/mm2 for 10 min. Fig. 6a shows the corresponding photothermal heating curve under different power densities. This result shows that the MnFe2O4 particles exhibit an adequate optical absorption when exposed at 980 nm laser irradiation, which causes a large increase of the temperature in solution. The photothermic energy conversion phenomenon could be due to a surface plasmon resonance of Fe−Mn binary oxide particles over the near-infrared region, as occurs with gold particles and some liquid metals such as gallium particles [73,74]. As can be seen, the linear segment of the curve up to approximately 250 ºC (Insert- Fig. 6a) is consistent with the surface plasmon resonance phenomenon, where the optical absorption of Fe−Mn binary oxide particles triggers electron−electron and electron−phonon scattering generating heat, which is transferred to the surroundings of the nanostructure [75,76]. We believe that the change in the linearity of the curve from 300 ºC and an optical density of 0.03 W · mm2 onwards is due to the fact that some heat dissipation occurs between the Fe−Mn binary oxide particles. In parallel, under the same conditions such as laser irradiation, power density and optical irradiation time, the measurements of temperature vs. optical density from the MnFe2O4@CDs composite were carried out to obtain its heating curve. In Fig. 6(a) as a comparison, it can be observed that the photothermal heating performance of the MnFe2O4@CDs composite is 20 % lower than that demonstrated by the Fe−Mn binary oxide particles. In this case, we believe that the decrease in the conversion of photothermal energy is due to certain dissipation of the optical and thermal energy between the MnFe2O4 particles and the carbonaceous material (CDs). For practical purposes the following photothermal conditions were kept constant, which we considered adequate for this study; a 980 nm NIR laser at an optical density of 0.025 W/ mm2, a focus distance of 30 mm, and laser intensity of 0.5 (a.u.); to maintain the reaction system at a temperature between 50 ºC and 60 ºC. Fig. 6b shows the infrared images collected with a thermal camera (Fluke Ti300). The NIR image shows a thermal mapping of MnFe2O4@CDs material colloidal under the appropriate irradiation conditions, where it is possible to observe that temperature of MnFe2O4 suspension rose quickly up to a maximum temperature of 53 °C ± 2 recorded by the thermal camera. It is worth mentioning that the sample of the material after reaching the maximum temperature in equilibrium for several cycles showed no deterioration in performance. To compare the photothermal properties of MnFe2O4 and MnFe2O4@CDs, a sample of each material was resuspended and characterized under the same conditions and the results for both samples were practically the same. Now, the ability to tune and optimize certain parameters such as optical density, focus distance, and laser intensity, shall benefit various applications of these materials, such as a photothermal heating agent for the photo-thermo-degradation of ATZN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the MnFe2O4@CDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of MnFe2O4@CDs composites were characterized by UV-Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy (FL) for the three materials: CDs, MnFe2O4 and MnFe2O4@CDs. Fig. 7a shows similar absorption peaks located at 338 nm and 410 nm, attributed to the CDs and that clearly remain constant also in the MnFe2O4@CDs composites. The first absorption band observed at 410 nm is assigned to a possible red shift of the functionalized CDs, associated to surface states. These n − π* electronic transitions are induced by functional groups such as −COOH and −COH [77]. The second band at 338 nm corresponds to π−π* transitions of the −CC conjugated system within sp 2 aromatic domains of the carbon core of CDs [78], but it is associated to the edge transitions too. On the other hand, n − π* edge transitions include the excitation of nonbonding electrons of O atoms in −COOH and −COH to the π* orbital of the hybridized C core [79]. Referring to the MnFe2O4 material sample, it presents a single band at 276 nm (Fig. 7a), characteristic for these Fe−Mn binary oxide particles. It should be noted that for practical purposes, this latter signal is not considered as a possible interference during photoluminescence studies for the detection of ATZN.

A sample of MnFe2O4@CDs materials in solution was excited within the range 350 nm – 400 nm to determine the wavelength where the best fluorescence response occurs. A series of fluorescence spectra were obtained, where a strong emission can be seen within the visible spectral range, shown in Fig. 7b. Fluorescence spectra exhibit dual emission characteristics for CDs carbonaceous materials. The main emission center is observed at 520 nm which is assigned to −COOH and −COH as surface states on CDs [80,81]. The spectral features remain at the same wavelength even when varying the excitation wavelength, which means that it is independent of the excitation energy, and consistent on being the result of surface states emission that follows the main electronic transition from the photon absorption with λ ≤ 370 nm. In the same spectra, another emission band appears at 426 nm. However, this signal is not considered as a possible interference in subsequent FL analysis, because this signal of lower FL intensity corresponds to the emission generated by the graphitic core of the CDs [27]. The inset of Fig. 7b shows the bright blue FL of the MnFe2O4@CDs in aqueous suspension when excited with a 365 nm UV lamp. Likewise, the profile of the total intensity of PL vs. excitation wavelength, obtained from the integral of the fluorescence of each emission spectrum is shown in Fig. 7c. As can be observed, the highest fluorescent intensity was obtained at an excitation wavelength of λEX= 375 nm; therefore, this was chosen as the optimal λEX for subsequent experiments in atrazine detection assays.

We propose a detection strategy to ATZN based on its effect on the CDs-based fluorescent (FL) probes, due of the interactions and formation of hydrogen bonds [82] between the oxygenated functional groups (−COOH and −COH) exposed by the CDs on the MnFe2O4@CDs composites and the amino groups (−NH−) corresponding to the ATZN molecule. In this sense, the fluorescence response of CDs-based FL probe towards ATZN has been investigated under the optimized conditions at pH 8.0 in which the oxygenated groups are deprotonated from proton exchange related to the dissociation of −COOH and −COH, which favors the formation of hydrogen bonds (Figure SI-6). At the same time at slightly basic pH making the stable fluorophores which is indicated by stable luminescence analytical signal at 520 nm(λex = 375 nm) of CDs. In Fig. 8a, there is an increase in FL intensity with the concentration of ATZN, with respect to the spectral emission intensity of the reference analytical signal at 520 nm(λex = 375 nm), F o, i.e., the signal “Turns on”. These phenomena “Turn-on” that the probe experiences on addition of ATZN is attributed to the ATZN at the surface favoring the emission through surface defects; also, certain aggregation could improve the quantum confinement effect of the functionalized CDs, inducing fluorescence enhancement [28,82]. Another possible explanation is that the structural rigidification of the ATZN/CDs aggregate restricts intramolecular motions and results in fluorescence emission enhancement, which is also known as “Turn on” of FL [83,84]. From the qualitative fluorescence signals at 520 nm, the linear plot was obtained for the relative Fl signal, F/F0, where F0 is the intensity without ATZN (Insert Fig. 8a). The linear regression values were calculated the limit of detection (LOD) for ATZN was found to be LOD = 126 nM ± 0.06. This same fluorescence study allowed us to estimate the maximum amount of ATZN that can be detected by the CDs-based fluorescent probes. The maximum concentration is 22.5 mM of ATZN, since adding aliquots of the analyte does not show a significant increase in the fluorescence of the analytical signal above this concentration.

The hydrogen bonding interaction of surface −COOH and −COH groups of CDs and ATZN makes the probe selective toward ATZN even in the presence of other herbicides, cations, and anions. To prove this, the selectivity fluorescence assay has been investigated taking PO4 3-, NO3 -, Cl-, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, cymoxanil, methiocarb, and imidacloprid as interfering analytes. A mixture in suspension at pH 8.0 of MnFe2O4@CDs fluorescence probe and ATZN (from 0.5 mM to 5.0 mM) was prepared. Within the same flask, each interfering analyte was added at double the concentration of ATZN and the FL intensity of analytical signal at 520 nm(λex = 375 nm) was measured. The FL emission curves show no significant shape changes after the addition of the interfering analytes. From these spectra, the percentage of interference was estimated for each interfering compound. Fig. 8b shows that anions and some cations (1+) only caused slight (≤ 2.5 %) change in FL peak intensities. With respect to presence of (2 +) metal ions, a considerable percentage (≤ 4.5 %) of FL quenching of the CDs probe was recorded. This is likely due to electrostatic interactions by difference in charges since the CDs and the surface moieties have a negative charge density that can attract metal cations. We further investigated the effect of other herbicides that could act as possible interferents in the FL “Turn-on” response of our CDs probe towards ATZN. In this case, there is a considerable variation of FL emission spectra after addition of cymoxanil (CYM) particularly, for which up to 9.0 % of interference was detected by this herbicide. We believe that this is because the CYM contains amino (−NH) groups, which somehow interact with the CDs by forming hydrogen bonds. However, this does not substantially affect the capture and detection process of ATZN. The other two herbicides considered shown much less interference percentage (≤ 2.0 %) in the detection assay of ATZN. These results shows that MnFe2O4@CDs is an adequate probe with high selectivity and sensitivity for FL responses to ATZN, over potential interferents.

This analytical fluorescence assay will allow detecting ATZN at low concentrations, and at the same time will make it possible to evaluate the capacity of the MnFe2O4@CDs composite to capture large concentrations of ATZN, which eventually within the same reaction system will be photothermally degraded taking into consideration the photothermal conversion capacity shown by the MnFe2O4 material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Atrazine capture-degradation process monitored by CDs-base fluorescence probes
ce:section.ce:para: The aggregation induced emission (AIE) has been used as a powerful tool in designing fluorescence “turn-on”/“turn off” as a molecular event, monitoring the increase and decrease in the FL intensity [82,85]. In this case, when ATZN comes into contact with CDs, adsorbs to the surface through hydrogen bonding, i.e., the “capture process” ( Fig. 9a). Fig. 9a shows the saturation curve for MnFe2O4@CDs composites towards the increases of ATZN concentration from 0 mM to 22.5 mM in a synthetic sample. This process shows that the MnFe2O4@CDs composites have the capacity to capture or anchor ATZN to up 20 mM on the CDs, this thanks to its greater specific surface area (Fig. 5d) which exposes a greater number of superficial active sites (−COO and −CO) that under constant stirring increased mass transfer of ATZN to the surface, promoting the significant “Turn-on” of fluorescence. In addition to verifying the efficiency in terms of the MnFe2O4@CDs material and the fluorometric method of capture and detection of ATZN, the feasibility of applying photothermal-activation of persulfate (PS) by MnFe2O4@CDs for the oxidation of ATZN also was studied. The montage used for the photo-thermo-degradation of ATZN is shown in the supplementary information (Figure SI-8). The degradation process was monitored in a reactor flask containing a suspension of MnFe2O4@CDs material, where aliquots of ATZN were added in increasing concentration. At the same time, the oxidant agent persulfate (PS) was introduced to the flask at increasing concentrations, to promote the generation of SO4 • − radicals. The results show that as the exposure time to the degradation process increases, it led to a reduced FL analytical signal at 520 nm (Fig. 9b). The photothermal effect takes place when the reactor is under constant irradiation from a NIR laser at 980 nm, this wavelength is absorbed by the Mn–Fe2O4 particles, and in turn, it is converted to heat, until reaching an equilibrium temperature that remained at 53 °C ± 2 (Fig. 6b).

In parallel, we evaluated the effect of persulfate (PS) concentration on ATZN degradation. Table 1 presents the data on the percentage of ATZN degradation recorded at 45 min as a function of the increase in the PS concentration. The values were extracted from the loss of fluorometric signal for each PS concentration (from 0.0 mM to 7.0 mM). The data provided in the Table 1 indicate that the increase in temperature in the reaction medium, in the absence of PS, can cause a slight degradation of ATZN. However, when PS is added, it can be seen that the percentage of ATZN degradation increases significantly. From this information, we consider that the concentration [5.0 mM] of PS is the optimal one to continue with the degradation study.

As can be seen in Fig. 9b, the degradation of ATZN occurs rapidly at the optimal parameters such as 5 mM PS and 53 °C ± 2, because after 15 min a little more than 50 % of the captured atrazine concentration degrades, and after 45 min the degradation of 99 % is practically achieved. The degradation process was allowed to continue until 75 min, at the end of this time, no fluorescence signal was observed, indicating a complete degradation of system CDs-ATZN as a whole. In Fig. 9c can be observed both processes, “capture-degradation” of ATZN through a progressive visual change in color. The color variation goes from the bright, regular, and distinguishable colors from deep blue (owing to CDs) to green and green-yellow transitions (owing to CDs-ATZN aduct) based on the increase in the concentration of ATZN. Subsequently, the aliquots taken during the degradation time show a progression from deep yellow-green to blue-green without reaching a deep blue. Furthermore, in the last two aliquots at 60 and 75 min the color completely disappears, which indicates, as expected, that the CDs-ATZN system was completely degraded.

Simultaneously we confirmed and validate the photo-thermo degradation of ATZN by analysis of total organic carbon (TOC) technique, which allowed us to evaluate the mineralization of ATZN [20 mM] before and after the degradation process. In this case we observed that the fluorometric analytical method implemented here reports 99 % degradation in 45 min. However the degradation reported by TOC technique as removed total organic carbon was only 77 % after 45 min ( Fig. 10), which suggests that the remaining percentage (23 %) of organic carbon present in the treated ATZN sample (insert in Fig. 10) was transformed into smaller molecules without reaching complete mineralization.

Now then, due that the degradation pathway of ATZN in our MnFe2O4@CDs-PS system is still unknown, we propose a possible reaction mechanism and the possible intermediate products generated, based on similar work previously reported. Fig. 11 show the reaction and activation mechanism of PS for ATZN degradation, which occurs on the surface of the MnFe2O4@CDs compound where the production of SO4 • − and •OH radicals is favored through two processes: 1) by chemical activation of the PS on the Mn–Fe2O4 and Fe3O4 particles where the iron acts as a redox couple Fe2+/Fe3+ facilitating the production of SO4 • − radicals through electron transfer from Fe(II) to PS according to the equation (Eq. 1) [34,36,86].

Meanwhile, the Mn could be acting as a redox mediator Mn2+/Mn3+ capable of redistributing the charge between Fe and Mn atoms [35]. This phenomenon promotes the constant reduction of Fe(III) into Fe(II) in the material regenerating the Fe(II) to activate the PS (Eq. 1) again and the consequent ATZN oxidation. In addition, 2) by photothermal activation of PS, which has been shown to be achieved when the PS in solution experiences to temperatures between 50 °C and 60 °C (Eq. 2) [87,88]. These SO4 • − radicals react with OH- and convert to •OH (Eq. 3) [89], but also the sulphate radicals may further react with H2O to generate •OH [34].

In this regard, the synergic effect between chemical and photothermal induction for PS activation causes an efficient and rapid degradation of ATZN (Fig. 9b) in the presence of both radicals such as SO4 • − and •OH. In order to complement the information, it is well known that the SO4 • − radicals have a strong affinity for ethyl and isopropyl groups and the ATZN contains these groups in its chemical structure, which satisfactorily increases its oxidative degradation [90]. In our case the SO4 • − and •OH attack towards ATZN result in its higher degradation up to a 77 % mineralized (Fig. 10), with 23 % of the remainder remaining in solution. According to similar works previously reported [34,41,91,92], in the remaining solution we could have the following molecules as the main intermediate products from ATZN degradation (Fig. 11). Among these products we find the DEIA especie which is formed in a first pathway by a continuous dealkylation of the ATZN. The DEIA is later converted to DEIHA by a dichlorination-hydroxylation-dealkylation process. Also, in a second pathway from ATZN the Hydroxyatrazine (HYA) is generated by a dichlorination-hydroxylation process. Then the dealkylation of HYA happened and formed product DEHA which undergoes a second dealkylation processes to form DEIHA as the final product. Finally, in a third pathway by a continuous process where it intervenes the oxidation of alkylamino lateral chain and the displacement of chloride by hydroxyl in the ATZN molecule led to the formation of 2-hydroxy-4-acetamido-6-isopropylamino-1,3,5,triazine (ODIT) and this molecule undergoes a final dealkylation step to form DEIHA. As described above, the DEIHA compound is the end product of the three main degradation pathways of ATZN. DEIHA can finally be transformed into cyanuric acid, which is more easily mineralized by breaking it down into CO2 and H2O [34,41].

After the ATZN degradation process was completed, we allowed the reactor to cool and the suspended metal particles to precipitate. Then we separated all the Mn–Fe2O4 particles content by magnetic method, washed thoroughly with a mixture of ethanol:H2O (1:1 v/v) and left to dry in an oven at 70 ºC for 3 h. The MnFe2O4@CDs composites were prepared again and the ATZN detection-capture and degradation process was carried out again. This was repeated four more cycles from the first use of the material. Fig. 12 shows the results obtained in terms of the mass of recovered Mn–Fe2O4 particles, their photothermal conversion performance, and the percentage capacity of ATZN capture and degradation. In Fig. 12a it can be observed that the recovered mass of Mn–Fe2O4 particles decreases slightly with respect to the increasing number of cycles of the process. We believe that this is due to the care during the magnetic separation, washing and drying of the metallic particles. Next, the photothermal conversion performance by the Mn–Fe2O4 particles was evaluated (Fig. 12b), and the infrared images collected with a thermal camera (Fluke Ti300) reports in each cycle the same temperature of ca. 53 °C, which is optimum for the thermoactivation of persulfate and the generation of SO4 • − radicals to ATZN degradation. Now, using the analytical method of fluorescence (% F/F0), we obtained the percentage curves for ATZN capture and degradation in each cycle. Fig. 12c shows a slight decrease in the percentage of ATZN capture capacity, this is because the amount of MnFe2O4@CDs composite was also lower in each cycle compared to the initial amount. However, the ATZN degradation percentage curve (Fig. 12d) remains efficient between 99 % for the first cycle and 98.5 % in the following four cycles. With these results we reveal that the Mn–Fe2O4 particles can be reused and/or recycled to consecutively manufacture the MnFe2O4@CDs composites, which preserve practically the same performance for the capture and degradation of ATZN under the optimal process conditions.

In order to make a comparison between our results and those previously reported by similar studies, we present the corresponding summary in Table 2, which shows the materials and methods used for the degradation of ATZN in aqueous media with certain efficiencies. In this regard, we have considered those works where hybrid iron materials are used. But also, the investigations where the thermal degradation study of ATZN is presented. Among the examples shown, it is possible to see that the composite iron materials doped with graphene [34], cobalt [35], cooper-zinc [40] or sulfides [36] have good degradation efficiencies for a maximum concentration of ATZN, in a range of 10 μM to 93 μM in relatively short times (10–30 min) with ATZN removals of up to 95 % in aqueous media. From these examples, it should be mentioned that the degradation are fast and of high percentages, although for low concentrations of ATZN. On the other hand, the degradation of ATZN by thermo-activation of the persulfate is also a relatively effective method by itself without the use of an additional activation material, reflecting a removal percentage of 67.7 % for a maximum concentration (50 ppm) of ATZN [41]. However, the factor to consider in this case is that this degradation process occurs after 2 h. For our case study, the application of the MnFe2O4@CDs composite as capture and photothermal activator agent for making SO4 • − radical from PS, allow to obtain a high yield (∼99.0 %) of ATZN degradation for a relatively large concentration of ATZN higher than 20 mM in aqueous media, in a period of 45 min. The materials and methods developed here satisfactorily impact the efficiency of the degradation process for high ATZN concentrations in lower time.

Herein, we have satisfactorily fabricated a novel MnFe2O4@CDs composite of dual behavior which has contributed to improve the efficiency of photothermal-degradation of Atrazine. This is attributed to its elemental composition and adequate structural morphology, high specific surface area, abundant active sites such as oxygenates functional groups, and excellent photothermal conversion, which are favorable properties for capture and degradation of Atrazine in aqueous media. We demonstrate the excellent performance of the proposed process for simultaneous capture-detection and degradation of Atrazine achieve 99 % effective degradation from initial concentrations of up to CATZN = 20 mM withih 45 min. The high percentage of degradation of the contaminant is due to the great oxidative capacity of the SO4 • − radicals generated in high concentrations by the photothermal activation of persulfate while the system reaches a temperature of ca. 53 °C ± 2. In addition the composite MnFe2O4@CDs also functions effectively as a fluorescent probe of single-channel through which it was possible to exhibit an easy fluorometric visualization and quantificacion of the capture and degradation of Atrazine. Therefore, we conclude that the fabrication of the MnFe2O4@CDs composites, the demonstrate fluorometric analytical method and the degradation photothermal process developed in the present work can be a new efficient, easy, and low-cost alternative for the capture, detection and degradation of Atrazine in groundwater and surface water samples, with the possible expansion to lixiviates to facilitate soil remidiation. Finally, it is important to mention the great relevance of the process in general because the MnFe2O4 particles can be magnetically recovered and coated again with CDs to be reused and/or recycled. After several cycles, the MnFe2O4@CDs composites are reused, maintaining practically the same performance for the degradation of Atrazine.
